Return to Transcripts main page

Voting Begins in New York: Will Mamdani's Candidates Win?; New Images Show Trump's Name Taken Off Kennedy Center; New Boss at Fed's First Priority: Fight Inflation. Aired 6-6:30a ET

Aired June 23, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

BRAD SMITH, CNN ANCHOR: And imagine opening your door and finding two million reasons to stay inside. That is exactly what happened in Texas after a truck carrying millions of bees crashed.

Video from Queen Bee Supply shows the chaos workers faced to capture the swarm. The company says that professional and backyard beekeepers stepped in to help.

That does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS. I'm Brad Smith. We're buzzing away. But CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Today in the group chat, it's primary day in New York. Can more Democratic socialists ride Mayor Mamdani's wave?

MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI (D), NEW YORK CITY: When does the race for 2028 begin? It starts now.

CORNISH: Anticipation builds to today's Supreme Court decisions. Will justices go against the president in cases that could help define his power?

And so-called deep-state firings have started at a top U.S. intelligence agency. What else could the president's hand-picked temporary director do?

And Tucker Carlson says he's out. Will he take MAGA with him?

TUCKER CARLSON, PODCASTER: I would not support the Republican Party. There's no chance I would support the Republican Party.

MAMDANI: Now, people often ask me what I think of the state of the Democratic Party. This slate here today is our answer. The Democratic Party must change.

CORNISH: Zohran Mamdani putting his power to the test. Can his team of Democratic socialists win big in New York?

Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and right now polls are open in New York. And the influence of New York City's mayor, tested just six months into his tenure.

He's backed candidates in three House races. Two are going up against Democratic incumbents, and they are hoping to ride the wave of Democratic socialists, who have been winning big races across the country.

BRAD LANDER (D), CANDIDATE, NY-10: I'm a longtime progressive, a Working Families Party member. But yes, if by "Democratic socialist," do you mean do I want Medicare for all? Do I want to make sure that folks can organize unions? Do I want to make sure we're getting to universal childcare and investing a lot more in housing everyone can afford? Yes.

DARIALIZA AVILA CHEVALIER (D), CANDIDATE, NY-13: I am someone who has felt deeply abandoned by the establishment politics that far too often see my community as merely a statistic.

CORNISH: All right, so today in the group chat, Tamara Keith, senior political correspondent at NPR; Chuck Rocha, Democratic strategist and former senior adviser to Bernie Sanders's presidential campaigns; and Ashley Davis, former White House official under President George W. Bush.

Love seeing you guys. Thank you so much for being here.

CHUCK ROCHA, DEMOCRATIC STRATEGIST: Of course.

CORNISH: So, Tamara, I want to start with you, because Mamdani story is a story that I think a lot of these folks would like to emulate. They're not handpicked by the party, yet somehow, they rise via the -- you know, the Internet and the power of their ideas. And he is trying to have, like, tailwinds. So, do we know if he does?

TAMARA KEITH, SENIOR POLITICAL CORRESPONDENT, NPR: I think we will know very soon whether he does. He -- certainly, some of these candidates look to be polling pretty well in their races.

I don't know that we can extrapolate about the national Democratic Party from what happens in New York City.

CORNISH: No, I mean, Democratic socialists.

KEITH: Right. CORNISH: Which, city by city, I mean, even here in D.C. --

CORNISH: -- we've been talking about that.

I was noticing in -- was that Lander who we just played earlier?

CORNISH: Notice how the first thing out of his mouth in that answer is, "If by Democratic socialist, you mean." And I hear this over and over again, people trying to define it for themselves, even as actual Democratic socialists are out there defining it pretty loudly.

ROCHA: I would go a step further and say that it's already seen.

In political world, you don't beat incumbents. You don't go against the machine. In city politics, New York City. To your point, Audie, in Washington, D.C., just last week, when three Democratic socialists overcame the odds in like --

ROCHA: All the way around.

ROCHA: but that's much different than every other battleground district in America. There's about 34 battleground districts, and none of those are in the heart of any city where Democratic socialists -- And that's what the party is worried about.

The Democratic socialist message of being for the people, by the people. Working class. Pull yourself up. That's all good. But in these marginal seats, they'll say everybody's a Democratic socialist. All Ashley's friends will be running ads tomorrow on this.

CORNISH: I know. You're trying to divorce it here. I want to play for you. This is Attorney General Tish James.

ROCHA: -- with it. I ain't divorcing nothing.

CORNISH: No, no, hold on one second, because this is going to be the difficult thing as a consultant.

CORNISH: How do you do this when there are people who are successfully running --

CORNISH: -- behind this flag? So basically, race was -- Vox was calling this race a splitting of Mamdani's coalition: that it pitted progressives against Democratic socialists. [06:05:02]

And then Letitia James was asked about this split. She's the former [SIC] attorney general. Here's what she had to say.

LETITIA JAMES, ATTORNEY GENERAL OF NEW YORK: What we are witnessing here at this moment is the tension. Dr. King talked about creative tension being a catalyst for social -- for social justice.

And clearly, given D.S. -- DSA and quote, unquote "progressive Democrats," we are moving forward, and we are broadening the tent of the Democratic Party.

We are moving forward, broadening the tent, focusing on women, people of color, immigrants, working people in the labor movement. And that's why -- that's what you are witnessing now, given the split between a mayor who I've supported and who is a friend of mine, who I respect.

CORNISH: And I should say current attorney general.

ASHLEY DAVIS, FORMER WHITE HOUSE OFFICIAL UNDER PRESIDENT GEORGE W. BUSH: Yes.

CORNISH: But I wanted to ask you how you hear her describing this split, which I think Bush-era Republicans may be familiar with. Right? When the party starts to pull away from you.

DAVIS: Well, right. I mean, I think this whole movement is two things. One, it's the Tea Party of the Republicans back in the day, or maybe even MAGA in some way.

But also, I think it's really anti-establishment, which most people in the country, whether you are Republicans or Democrats, are 100 percent running against the status quo, because no one's happy with it.

But what's really going to show me for the mayor, because I think that these -- he has a really good chance of winning these three races today. At least two of them.

But can they win in a general? And I think that's the question.

DAVIS: I think we'll see. Some will, some won't.

CORNISH: Let me just play one last thing. This is the mayor. Mayor Zohran Mamdani. He was just speaking from the mayor's office on Monday.

MAMDANI: I was quoting Gramsci, who said that the old world is dying and the new world struggles to be born. Now is the time of monsters.

I used the term to describe all those who are preventing the birth of a new world. Not solely AIPAC, but frankly, super PACs at large who are spending millions of dollars in deceptive and misleading ads that are blanketing airwaves.

CORNISH: I wanted to add this one last thing. We're going to talk about it more in the program.

But this conversation around AIPAC and how it is dividing the Democratic Party and, to some extent, even Republicans. I remember Tom Massie's loss in Kentucky. He vehemently criticized AIPAC after.

KEITH: Yes. And AIPAC has, in the last several years, become a real boogeyman in races. This obviously has a lot to do with politics involving Israel and Israel's shift --

CORNISH: And exacerbated by October 7th and all that has followed since.

KEITH: Absolutely. And everything that has followed.

KEITH: And it has created a political rift, both in the Democratic Party and in the Republican Party. And this backlash, you know, against Israel, which is a change.

CORNISH: Yes. And that thread runs through Tucker Carlson's exit, as --

KEITH: It runs all the way from Tucker Carlson to Zohran Mamdani.

CORNISH: Exactly. So, we're going to talk more about that on the program today. We've got a lot to handle.

I do want to bring up one other thing, coming up on CNN THIS MORNING. There's actually a new clue in the Nancy Guthrie case. What it could say about her disappearance.

Plus, what's behind the tarp outside the Kennedy Center? New images showing what we now know is not there.

And we're remembering the man with the golden ears who launched the careers of icons, including Whitney Houston and Bruce Springsteen. Legendary music producer Clive Davis has died at the age of 94. Here he is in 2024, talking about his legacy.

CLIVE DAVIS, MUSIC PRODUCER: I discovered or nurtured an unusual array of the most gifted artists of all time and that they felt safe.

(END VIDEO CLIP) (COMMERCIAL BREAK)

CORNISH: It is now 12 minutes past the hour. This is your morning roundup.

Law enforcement sources telling CNN a note connected to the disappearance of Nancy Guthrie claims that she died, and that note was sent to media outlets back in February.

We're told the kidnappers wrote it -- wrote that she died shortly after she was taken and that they did not mean to kill her.

Now, this note followed one prior which demanded ransom. Investigators believe these notes are legitimate.

And a major ruling on voting and privacy. The Trump administration blocked from using Social Security and immigration data to scrub voter rolls.

A federal judge says the system relied on inaccurate information and risks wrongly removing eligible voters.

Now, this ruling halted a key part of the president -- of the president's election overhaul plan.

And we're getting a new look at what's behind the tarp at the Kennedy Center. These images, obtained by CNN, show President Trump's name is no longer on the building.

Workers began removing Trump's name from an exterior wall ten days ago, complying with a judge's ruling that the institution could only be named for John F. Kennedy as a living memorial to the late president.

Ashley, I feel like you have some goss here. You're involved with the National Symphony?

DAVIS: Yes, absolutely. And I think it's really important, because the Kennedy Center -- also, the judge said the Kennedy Center will remain open for now, as well. And we'll see what happens there.

CORNISH: Instead of shutting down for a big Trump-induced renovation.

DAVIS: Exactly. But the NSO is in to stay. And they are either going to do it with the symphony or some other way, if there's not going to be a budget to support them.

But 100 percent, these musicians have stayed with the NSO from the beginning, and they --

CORNISH: None of them quit during --

DAVIS: None of them quit.

CORNISH: -- this big upheaval.

DAVIS: And we have a season to announce as soon as we can, and --

ROCHA: What's the NSO?

DAVIS: National Symphony Orchestra.

ROCHA: That's why I don't know what the NSO is.

CORNISH: I'm going to take you to a concert. I'm going to take you to a concert.

CORNISH: Shout-out to the National Symphony orchestra. Glad you guys stuck with it. Looking forward to the new season.

I want to talk about this after the break. Does a quieter Fed equal a louder market? How Kevin Warsh's gamble might impact your money.

Plus, the light at the end of the tunnel is here for Americans exposed on that hantavirus-stricken cruise ship.

In the meantime, a live look in Houston. The World Cup play will continue there with a match featuring Portugal versus Uzbekistan.

KEVIN WARSH, FEDERAL RESERVE CHAIRMAN: I don't share the view that was expressed a few generations ago that Federal Reserve chairmen show up at a podium like this and say, you've got to choose, and you're going to have to decide whether you're willing to tolerate higher inflation to put more people at work.

CORNISH: Newly sworn-in Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh, leaving interest rates steady for now. But concerning economic data, since the start of the war with Iran could mean he'll have to change his mind.

So let me give you an example. Inflation rose half a percent from April to May. That means it's now around 4.2 percent. Consumer confidence dipping among Americans.

So, joining me now Courtenay Brown. She's the senior economics reporter at Axios. Welcome back. Thanks so much.

So, I'm starting with the inflation, just to give people a sense, because that's always the boogeyman. Is inflation high? Is inflation low? And this is where monetary policy comes into play. What is our sense, now that Warsh is in the job, of how he thinks

COURTENAY BROWN, SENIOR ECONOMICS REPORTER, AXIOS: Do you want to hear something crazy?

CORNISH: About Fed policy, Courtenay. Yes.

BROWN: Because he is not so -- Warsh's big audition was last week. First Fed policy meeting. We get the closely parsed policy statement. It is 130 words, more than a third of the length in the Powell era, just months before.

He wants to say less. He wants to guide markets less.

What we know is that he said that inflation -- or the Fed will accomplish price stability. A wonky way of saying they will get inflation back to 2 percent. As you said, so far from 2 percent right now.

CORNISH: Yes. Can I -- let's look at the chart. She was making fun of me trying to read my own charts here for business.

BROWN: I like your charts.

CORNISH: Consumer price index.

CORNISH: OK. What are we looking at in this chart right now?

BROWN: So, what we've seen is inflation shooting right up since the war.

CORNISH: Oh, see, I did the same thing guys. That's confidence. Give me consumer price.

BROWN: But that's an important story, too.

CORNISH: Oh, it is? Why?

BROWN: The stories are connected.

BROWN: Because inflation is shooting up, consumers feel really crappy about the economy right now. We all know this. Not a new story. It is a -- it is partially because of inflation, but it's also because

of price levels. Prices have just been really high and have not come down.

So, I think what the Fed has to grapple with here is how do you bring inflation down without wrecking the economy? Financial markets have decided that the Fed will probably raise interest rates this year. That's crazy.

CORNISH: "Wall Street Journal" saying Fed could raise -- could raise it three times this year.

CORNISH: Pointing to --

BROWN: Everyone on Wall Street --

CORNISH: -- reporting from the forecast from Bank of America.

BROWN: Everyone on Wall Street is revising their expectations for rate hikes this year. When just a few months ago, they were expecting rate cuts.

But we don't actually know. Kevin Warsh didn't say that. He doesn't want to guide markets in the way that traditional Fed chairman have. He wants to go back to the Greenspan era, the late Alan Greenspan, who died yesterday.

CORNISH: Let's take a beat on that.

CORNISH: Because there's some familiar language here. When the -- the late Alan Greenspan, who died this week, when he took over the Fed from Paul Volcker, you had Gulf wars fueling spikes in prices, right? So, something going on in the Middle East.

You had high inflation, and you had real worries about that and prices. So, some of this feels a bit familiar for us in this moment under Trump's term.

What did Greenspan do that's part of his legacy, why people still think of him with -- with such reverence?

BROWN: The celebrity Fed chairman of our era, really.

There's one more parallel to this moment that's quite spooky. Alan Greenspan, during his term, he presided -- or he led the Fed over a period where there was huge technological change, the Internet boom of the '90s.

CORNISH: Oh, yes. And its bubble.

BROWN: Yes. Sound like A.I. a little bit right now?

BROWN: Maybe. So, I think Alan Greenspan is having such a moment right now. It is so interesting to me that he died at the -- at the moment where his legacy is kind of front and center --

BROWN: -- among Trump economic officials. You hear them shouting out Alan Greenspan because of what he did.

He -- he believed that, then the Internet boom was going to usher in this huge era of productivity. And it would not be inflationary.

And we've heard the White House explicitly say that they think the Fed can do the very same thing in this moment: keep interest rates low, not cut off this economic boom that we're having.

BROWN: But the problem is exactly what you say. We have this inflation issue. It's really hard for the Fed not to raise interest rates in the face of inflation that is soaring now, but was already sticky before the war. So, they have a problem.

CORNISH: One other thing I want to read from Alan Greenspan, because I think it's relevant here.

In 1994, he said, "We will be at price stability when households and businesses need not factor expectations of changes in the average level of prices into their decisions."

I'm going to translate and say he cared about our prices, about how households were able to afford their economy.

BROWN: He did. He did.

CORNISH: Courtenay, thank you so much. Love having you. I'm going to come up with more excuses to cover Fed policy just to soak in your enthusiasm.

Next on CNN THIS MORNING, we're going to talk about the issues from transgender athletes to birthright citizenship before the Supreme Court. We could get some opinions later today, before its summer recess.

Plus, mass firings underway at the nation's top intel office. And lawmakers warn of serious risks with each lost job.

segments.title: CNN.com - Transcripts
segments.text:
Return to Transcripts main page

Supreme Court Releases Opinions Today; Firings Begin Under Pulte; Errol Louis is Interviewed about the AIPAC and Israel Rift; Carlson Quits the GOP. Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired June 23, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish. Thank you for joining me on CNN THIS MORNING.

It is half past the hour. And here's what's happening right now.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio heads to the Middle East. He is the first White House official to do so after the signing of the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding. Rubio will be in the region discussing that deal, along with, quote, regional priorities, including the Strait of Hormuz.

And quarantine finally over for the last eight American passengers exposed to hantavirus who were staying at a Nebraska facility. Now, they were exposed to that virus on a cruise ship early last month. The passengers stayed in the quarantine unit for 42 days.

And Messi makes history again. He scored two goals in Argentina's win over Austria, officially earning him the most goals scored in World Cup history, 18.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: Messi. Messi's a beast. Messi came to get the record of most goals in the World Cup, and he got it. He got two of them.

CORNISH: But Messi will be fighting to keep the record. France, on the right, on his heels with 16 all-time goals.

And this morning the Supreme Court expected to release some opinions for its outstanding docket. So, you've got cases still left outstanding, those include birthright citizenship. Also, presidential firing authority and transgender athletes. These are all opinions the Trump administration has a vested interest in.

And Trump has already lashed out against the Supreme Court over decisions relating to, say, his tariff policy. So, could these opinions further erode the relationship between the executive and judiciary?

The group chat is back.

The big one here I want to mention is birthright citizenship. This could really be something that would annoy Trump. And, honestly, it's not looking that good because it's one of those things where it's kind of in the Constitution. What are you thinking about how he's feeling?

TAMARA KEITH, HOST, "NPR POLITICS PODCAST": Yes, it was one of his day one priorities in his second term. It's something that he campaigned on. It's also something that legal experts have been extremely dubious about the entire time.

KEITH: And I do think that we could expect him, if it goes against him, to be very unhappy and not to be quiet about that because he is never quiet when he is unhappy. And you saw that with the tariff ruling. And, you know, like the relationship between --

KEITH: I mean, like even talking about it as a relationship anymore --

CORNISH: But even many of these things, out of all the -- everything on the list, I feel like the transgender athlete case is the one that becomes a talking point in elections, right? In this case, it's two students challenging state laws, banning participation in -- their participation in girl's sports, arguing that it violates Title IX. Whereas like, the abortion ruling, so many years ago, became this, like, pivotal thing for Democrats. Out of everything here, mail-in ballots, birthright citizenship, it feels like you're going to see ads about a transgender athlete ruling.

ASHLEY DAVIS, FORMER HOMELAND SECURITY OFFICIAL: It only becomes an issue if they say that you can have the transgender athletes in the sports. I think that if they say, no, you can't, I don't know, are Democrats going to defend that?

CHUCK ROCHA, DEMOCRATIC STRATEGIST: I think that you're already seeing Republicans use transgender, whether or not the Supreme Court does this or not, you're going to see two types of ads, and we're making hundreds of ads right now in these congressional seats. And you have Democrats talking about cost of living, and you have Republicans talking about trans athletes or somebody having low t. That's going to be the argument of -- we're seeing what the midterms --

CORNISH: Low t. The low t, testosterone, conversation.

ROCHA: I like that. Do you like that?

CORNISH: Why is it in politics?

ROCHA: I'm just telling you what they got.

CORNISH: It's like, guys, just save it for your supplements. I don't need --

ROCHA: If you ain't got nothing else.

DAVIS: They're talking about low t?

ROCHA: I'm just saying, as a boy who takes a lot of protein, this is what they're doing and it's going to lose momentum (ph).

CORNISH: That's what they're calling Talarico. That's what they're calling Talarico. Which I get, but just the whole --

DAVIS: But it worked really well last time. I don't know if it's still the same.

ROCHA: It's a different time.

DAVIS: Unless there's a bad ruling or a --

ROCHA: People are sick and tired right now. They're sick and tired of these prices. I just went home to see my mama this weekend for her 74th birthday, and only anything could say is that folks can't afford stuff.

CORNISH: But, Chuck, isn't the argument that Democrats, too, are not focusing on the right things because they are focused on their affinity, communities or not eating steak.

ROCHA: Smart people like you want to talk about focus.

CORNISH: No, no, what I'm saying is that's --

ROCHA: I'm talking about ads.

CORNISH: OK, don't ads generate focus? You -- they're determining where to look.

CORNISH: And I think Republicans are going to say, don't look over here where they're talking about affordability, look over here.

ROCHA: You're right. That's right.

DAVIS: But that's what they're running against Talarico with your buddy on is they're going to go over some of these issues --

ROCHA: Because they can't talk about what Audie just said, which is the cost of things.

DAVIS: I know, but -- ROCHA: You can't talk about that. But you talk about something else.

DAVIS: Some of those ads are effective, I'm sorry to say.

ROCHA: Some of them with the base.

DAVIS: Well, one other thing I want to add is, there is this idea, a Senate committee advancing a bipartisan bill to allow oral arguments to be televised, saying that this would be good for transparency. "The Washington Post" editorial board joining them. Or, sorry, "The Washington Post" editorial board complaining that this will contribute to grandstanding.

You're laughing. I mean, I have to admit, I'm good with audio.

KEITH: I -- yes, I'm, as a (INAUDIBLE), I'm thrilled with the audio.

CORNISH: As audio people, we're happy already.

KEITH: Very happy already.

I don't know that you can grandstand in the Supreme Court because I don't think the justices will stand for that. They are asking deep dive questions about very specific things.

CORNISH: But I think what they can -- what they say can be clipped and used against people in election slop. Like, I don't think --

DAVIS: Yes, but they're not going to bother.

CORNISH: People are going to use those same televised clips to make a thing.

ROCHA: And we'll use them. We'll use an audio ad overlay pictures all day long in a TV ad.

ROCHA: Especially if they -- yes.

CORNISH: All right, I want to turn to something else that's just a little bit more important, especially since we're going to have those rulings later today.

There is a source telling CNN that the so-called, quote, deep state firings have begun at the Office of the Director of National Intelligence. Here's why we're watching this. President Trump's new acting spy chief, Bill Pult, he's actually in charge already, right? Not -- this hasn't been confirmed or anything like that. He's interim. And Democrats are raising concerns.

SEN. CHRIS VAN HOLLEN (D-MD): Bill Pulte is somebody who has zero, zero experience when it comes to national security and intelligence matters. His sort of defining mission in the Trump administration has been as a political hit man for Donald Trump to go after his political adversaries. And now this is the guy who's going to be firing people with experience and expertise when it comes to our national security intelligence matters. So, what could go wrong?

CORNISH: OK, so here's the thing. I'm not one for the 3-D chess argument. But this is the person Trump wants. Bill Pulte, who comes from housing policy, that's not the person that anyone in the Senate seems to want.

KEITH: Including Republicans.

CORNISH: Including Republicans. Let's play an example. Lindsey Graham, this Sunday on CBS, complaining that the president wasn't letting Jay Clayton, the person they actually feel like has the votes, testify.

SEN. LINDSEY GRAHAM (R-SC): And I would urge President Trump to let Clayton testify. And if we nominate Clayton and get him affirmed as the new DNI, we don't want FISA to go dark. There's too many threats to our country for 702 to go down. Anybody who owns the shutting down of 702 under FISA will own a future attack against the United States.

CORNISH: So, there is this FISA surveillance law that allows the government to go after intelligence targets abroad and inadvertently maybe capture some U.S. information. The point is, his need to have Pulte looks like it's starting to hold up other aspects of the administration's agenda.

KEITH: Yes, it is. But President Trump has been very focused for a very long time about retribution, whatever you want to call it. He feels like the intelligence community went after him in 2016. Russia, Russia, Russia. He feels like his first impeachment was the result of the intelligence community. And Bill Pulte is there to do a job.

CORNISH: I think what's shady to me is that --

KEITH: Like to get rid of these --

CORNISH: Of course. But he's interim. Like, he has Clayton right there. He could put someone forth that Republicans are willing to give him the votes for. And instead, just to give the reviews here, here is what some of these Republicans have been saying over the last couple of weeks when Pulte's name was raised.

SEN. THOM TILLIS (R-NC): I think he's the worst form of sycophant and advisor to the president that is going to hurt the president's legacy.

SEN. JOHN CORNYN (R-TX): I see no, no evidence of any qualifications for that job.

SEN. KEVIN CRAMER (R-ND): I don't know Jay Clayton, but I was quite certain the acting guy wasn't going to cut it, shall we say, Pulte?

SEN. JOHN KENNEDY (R-LA): What's always worried me about Mr. Pulte, he doesn't have a real deep background in intelligence.

MANU RAJU, CNN CHIEF CONGRESSIONAL CORRESPONDENT: And Pulte, Pulte's (INAUDIBLE).

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: I have no observations on the matter.

CORNISH: I'm going to start using that one, actually. No, keep in mind, this person --

DAVIS: Actually, they (ph) just laugh after he said that. Did you see that?

CORNISH: That the person they're describing is now already firing people.

DAVIS: Well, I'm OK with actually -- like taking everything else out of it. There's so many things here.

First of all, I'm hoping that Clayton issue gets solved tomorrow when the president goes up and talks to Senate Republicans because I actually think that is part of a broader issue. Pulte is in there for one thing, as everyone said, which is to get rid of a lot of these people. I was around when we created this agency, the DNI. I think that there's a lot of issues with it, and there's a lot of fat, but there's also a lot of leaks. The problem is, is Bill is a little bit -- not a little bit. Bill is a lightning rod because he doesn't have any internet (ph). He doesn't have any background in intelligence.

CORNISH: Is that the only reason it's a lightning rod, Ashley?

DAVIS: Well, no. He's also a lightning rod just because of all of his policies. I mean he actually did a lot of cage rattling within the -- in the West Wing. So, there is -- there's not a big -- there's not a -- he has a lot of enemies and a lot of allies, but his number one -- the number --

ROCHA: Didn't somebody already hit him at the White House? Didn't somebody punch him in the face?

DAVIS: No, that wasn't -- that was -- that was Secretary Bessent.

CORNISH: Oh, that was --

ROCHA: Oh, my bad. KEITH: And he just threatened to.

CORNISH: Yes, just threatened. Yes.

ROCHA: I get my MMA mixed up all the time at the White House.

DAVIS: But just -- I just want to say something, like, yes, that's true.

ROCHA: I'll be quite now.

DAVIS: There is something that's really important. The DNI agency is the most leaked agency in the intelligence world. There needs to be changes there. It's just, the controversy is around the person that's doing it.

CORNISH: Should a person who doesn't have the job yet, is not confirmed by the Senate, be firing people already? That seems like it opens the door for people --

DAVIS: If they're leaking stuff, I don't care who fires them.

CORNISH: But we don't know why. That's the thing.

DAVIS: Well, there's major investigations going on that are classified of who's leaking. So, yes, I think that if -- those people should be fired if they're leaking national --

ROCHA: I think Trump's going to get exactly what he wants. He wants this.

ROCHA: Trump wants Pulte firing everybody. And then he wants to say, OK, we got rid of that bad guy. We'll bring Clayton in. Everybody likes this Clayton guy because they put up the worst of the worst of somebody that's totally not qualified to do this job.

CORNISH: It seems like the recipe for a lawsuit. But, like, I'm not a lawyer. I don't know.

DAVIS: But I think -- well, I'm -- I mean, that's like the everyone is --

CORNISH: Do you know what I mean? Like, it kind of feels like, are you fired without cause? Like, can you raise the question?

DAVIS: I like to -- I'm making my prediction. I think Clayton's confirmed in July.

CORNISH: I think he's -- you're both right. He's confirmed. But after a bunch of firings.

DAVIS: No, in July. I think he gets confirmed in July. ROCHA: (INAUDIBLE). Tell her (INAUDIBLE). Here we go.

CORNISH: OK, you guys, there's one more. I want to talk about this. This is actually really interesting.

Next on CNN THIS MORNING, Tucker Carlson claiming he's out of the Republican Party. This is years after defending Trump to the MAGA movement. So, we're wondering where he's going to take his support next. And then later on CNN, a White House plot grows. There are new arrests revealing drones, explosives and a widening network connected with the alleged plot to attack the UFC event.

CORNISH: So, the Democratic Party, as we talked about, continues to struggle with its messaging on Israel in the wake of the '24 election. Today, as voters in New York head to the polls, a pro-Israeli lobbying group highlights a rift in the party. AIPAC, American Israel Public Affairs Committee, it's donated nearly $42 million to campaigns this year alone. And several high-profile Democrats, Cory Booker, Gavin Newsom, AOC, say they refuse to accept AIPAC funds. In New York, Mayor Zohran Mamdani also calling out candidates who do not take that money -- candidates who do take that money.

MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI (D), NEW YORK CITY: The old world is dying and the new world struggles to be born. Now is the time of monsters. These monsters take many forms today.

In AIPAC, for whom the only thing more frightening than democracy being allowed to run its course is an end to genocide in Netanyahu's wars.

They move millions in dark money to accomplish a single goal, to preserve their power so that they can turn us against one another, instead of our leaders turning towards the moral change we all know to be necessary.

CORNISH: OK. He was specifically pointing to Congressman Adriano Espaillat, who is running against one of Mamdani's backed candidates.

Now, according to the Federal Election Commission, AIPAC made two donations last month totaling around $600,000. Ted Deutch, the CEO of the American Jewish Committee, called Mamdani's comments, quote, "outrageous and dangerous, and the impact of your words extend far beyond politics."

Mamdani has doubled down.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP) MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI (D), NEW YORK: I want to be very clear. We're talking about a status quo where children are being killed on a daily basis.

And when I am speaking about AIPAC, I'm speaking about an organization that has been supportive of the status quo, that has fought any attempt to actually deliver safety to people, not just in Palestine, but, frankly, through much of the region. And it is a status quo for immorality. It is one that I will not accept.

And I think that it is important that when we ask ourselves how such death and destruction is happening overseas, we also name those who allow it to take place.

CORNISH: Joining me now, Errol Louis, political anchor with Spectrum News.

Errol, in a minute I want to play for you Rep, Dan Goldman, who had kind of an altercation with a coffee shop around this issue of anti- Semitism.

But first, can you talk about how this AIPAC conversation, why it's playing such a big role in these essentially small city races?

ERROL LOUIS, POLITICAL ANCHOR, SPECTRUM NEWS: Yes. Good morning, Audie.

The issue of AIPAC is really shorthand for opposing Israel's policies in Gaza and elsewhere in the region.

And so, they've done what politicians do with slogans. They've made it so that it basically ends the conversation just to say, hey, you took money from AIPAC and then you can't say much more about it. And that's, you know, this is -- this is what happens in the middle of a heated campaign.

It's a little unusual because just as the mayor talks about this and has made it really central to a lot of his politics and a lot of the politics of these congressional races, there are a ton of other issues. I mean we're always talking about affordability, and somehow this issue has taken what some would call an outsized level of influence when it comes to all of these local races.

CORNISH: Is it also because culturally what's happened on the streets, even since Mamdani's election, right, since October 7th, but then since Mamdani's election itself, when you had people saying, oh, we're going to leave the city if he wins, right, raising over these concerns.

In particular, I want to flag for you something that happened downtown, I think it was Brooklyn. I'm not sure.

CORNISH: And you've got this guy, Congressman Goldman. And he's dealing with a bit of a firestorm. He was visiting a coffee shop there. And after he visited, they posted that he was banned over his support for Israel. And they wrote, "we see that you stopped by our shop for coffee. Do you see how it doesn't taste like genocide juice? Or are you still having a hard time telling the difference?" They go on to say, "we don't serve racist, fascists, homophobes, genocide enablers, or anyone in between. Too bad we didn't recognize you right away."

So, Goldman responded. And here's what he had to say.

REP. DAN GOLDMAN (D-NY): And I think it's really dangerous when people start conflating the actions of the Israeli government with American Jews who have absolutely nothing to do with what is going on in Israel and the rise in anti-Semitism, because of what is happening in the Middle East, here in the United States, is really dangerous and it is escalating significantly.

CORNISH: Errol, I don't know if you had heard about this incident --

CORNISH: But what do you make of how he is, you know, articulating this?

LOUIS: Yes, I did hear about the incident. It's not far from where I'm sitting right now, in fact, in Brooklyn. And very disturbing. Very disturbing. This was -- he went in because he needed to use the bathroom. He was with his seven-year-old daughter while he was out campaigning. And they were nice enough to let him do it. And he bought a cup of coffee. And then after the fact, they published this photo and that statement and that kind of a thing.

Listen, the issues in the Middle East are so complicated. Issues like genocide, fascism, you know, these buzzwords that some of these activists like to throw around with such abandon are the opposite of trying to find some kind of a path forward and some kind of a civil discourse, which is hopefully what you'll have, including during a political campaign of this kind. It's a real -- really a lost opportunity.

But make no mistake about it, this is a group of activists who have declared war on the mainstream Democratic Party. And they are using these buzzwords, they are using these issues, that kind of rough style where they try and target people and personalize it is what they're trying to bring to politics. And they say that this is going to be the path forward. We'll see later today, tonight, whether or not New York voters are going to go for that.

CORNISH: That's Errol Louis. As always, totally appreciate your time. Thank you.

So, we've been talking about this issue, whether this could affect the fate of these politicians in New York. And it appears Israel sealed the fate of Tucker Carlson as well. For months, Republicans have been painting him as anti-Semitic.

SEN. TED CRUZ (R-TX): And I will say on the right, I have seen more anti-Semitism in the last 18 months on the right.

It is being spread by loud voices, the most consequential of whom is Tucker Carlson. I believe Tucker Carlson is the single most dangerous demagogue in this country.

CORNISH: Carlson says he is done with the Republican Party. He says it puts the interest of Israel above those of its own citizens.

TUCKER CARLSON, CONSERVATIVE POLITICAL COMMENTATOR: I would not support the Republican Party. There's no chance I would support the Republican Party. I'm not going to support the Democratic Party. I don't know what I'm going to do. But at this point, you know, how could you support -- how could I or any American voter support a political party that's not loyal to the United States?

CORNISH: Group chat is back.

Tam, we're going two ends of the spectrum here, right?

KEITH: And then it meets up at the top.

CORNISH: The horseshoe politics. I think what's interesting is that Tucker has been at odds with the White House for many months almost every time there's a foreign intervention. But this one in particular seems to have pushed him over the edge in terms of how he speaks publicly.

KEITH: I think that what is happening here with Tucker Carlson, with Ted Cruz is various parts of what has been the Republican coalition figuring out what happens in a potential post-Trump reality and positioning themselves.

And there are flashpoints. Israel is going to be a flashpoint. It didn't used to be. Republicans were all very pro-Israel. That's not the case anymore. And I think you're going to see it over abortion and other issues as well.

CORNISH: Yes. Marjorie Taylor Greene tweeting, "Tucker's not the only one who's done supporting the Republican Party." This is her, I think, on X. "There's a lot of us that are absolutely fed up and will not support a party that betrays its voters and country. That doesn't mean we're turning into Democrats either. But we are done with the America last Republican Party."

ROCHA: There's something going on here. It's not just this issue. If you think about this issue, you think about the war. You think about the Epstein files. You have all of these activists on the right who voted for Donald Trump because of those three things. And Tucker Carlson, major -- maybe Marjorie Taylor Greene, are trying to tap into that. The old political consultant here thinks it has a lot to do with 2028.

CORNISH: And, yes, 2028, and also where the public sentiment is. I mean when I went to look at the Pew research polls, I was trying to figure out, OK, what are the views of Israel right now based on the polling?

CORNISH: And in 2022, Republicans were at 27 percent with an unfavorable view of Israel. Now they're at 41, which means it's almost doubled. And meanwhile, Democrats, of course, we know, in 2022, 53 percent. Now they're at 80 percent. So, a Mamdani or a Tucker, they're speaking to the people who moved in this shift from 2022 to now.

DAVIS: Israel is what they're fighting right now. But I think it's very important to say they're against any sort of foreign conflict.

DAVIS: I don't think it's just Israel. I think it would be, if we were -- obviously, no one on the Republican side wanted us to be in Ukraine either. So, I think that's -- but I agree with Chuck in regards to --

CORNISH: But he's not leaving over Venezuela. Like, it feels as though --

DAVIS: No, but we're not at war --

DAVIS: I mean that was like something very quick. We don't have a lot of troops on the ground. You know, we're not -- anyway, I think that's a little bit different. But I get your point.

I also think that Marjorie Taylor Greene and Tucker are trying to figure out a path in regards to 2028. I think they're both testing the grounds of running. But also one of the questions I have really for you is, there are four or five potential Jewish candidates on the Democrat side that would run in 2028.

CORNISH: Correct. DAVIS: One of them, my dear friend Josh Shapiro. How does that happen? I mean, they're like going -- trying -- the Dems are trying to go left of the left of the left of Israel.

ROCHA: This has -- this has relevance in Democratic and primaries for both parties in 2028. When you have multiple people running, more than four or five, you can win the nomination with 30 percent. Keep in mind, you just need that 30 percent. And Tucker Carlson knows that.

CORNISH: OK. You get -- you think he's -- this is a he's running situation?

OK. Well, let's talk about what else is in our group chats.

CORNISH: Starting with you, Ashley.

DAVIS: I'm talking about the power pivot. OK, I just want to talk about how great it is to watch all of these people visiting from Europe or around the world for the soccer teams.

CORNISH: Yes. World Cup fans.

DAVIS: I mean, but also loving America. I mean they're like really loving our food. I have heard that a lot of people are gaining some weight.

ROCHA: We are making them fat.

DAVIS: So, we may need to give them some (INAUDIBLE) on the way out.

CORNISH: I had not heard that. But, thank you. Ashley.

DAVIS: Oh, yes. No, people are gaining lots of weight. You know, if you're in the Midwest, you're eating a lot of gravy.

Wait until you see until they get to Costco. That's all their minds will be blown.

But it is nice. It's been a lot of compliments.

KEITH: Just wait till you see -- until they get to Costco and their minds will be blown.

CORNISH: Oh, that's a whole other situation. Yes.

KEITH: But it is nice. It's been a lot of complements.

ROCHA: So today I'm really honored. I get to host the first annual Latino Vote Summit. We're bringing in Republican, independent, and Democrat Latinos to the press club today where we're going to talk about the Latino agenda. If you're a Republican, holding the Republican Party accountable. If you're a Democrat, holding -- but what is it for the community?

ROCHA: So, I'm really happy that we're coming together.

CORNISH: Because 2024 revealed some big rifts.

ROCHA: The Latino vote has probably been the one electorate that swung right, swung left, and now they're in the middle looking for a home. And I'm honored to be hosting this with my Latino vote podcast co-host Mike Madrid.

CORNISH: Very nice. Very nice. Shout out to the pod.

ROCHA: Oh, yes, finally. Thank you, Tamara.

CORNISH: Reflecting Pool.

KEITH: Yes. What I cannot let go of is this Reflecting Pool. The ongoing battle. The --

CORNISH: Are you hunting for vandals?

KEITH: I am not hunting for vandals.

CORNISH: Is Bill Pulte hunting for vandals?

KEITH: It could be part of the intel job. Who knows?

CORNISH: I don't know.

KEITH: It could be part of the remit.

KEITH: But, you know, finding out what happened. Epoxy. It's tough. So is keeping a pool clean.

CORNISH: Oh, really? Do you have a problem with this?

KEITH: I no longer have a pool.

DAVIS: Was it -- like what --

ROCHA: Did you say the president (ph)?

DAVIS: What happened to it?

CORNISH: You have to clean a pool. It's annoying.

DAVIS: I know. And you didn't. Is that why you got rid of it? Was it like an above ground swimming pool?

KEITH: It was -- it was just a lot of work.

CORNISH: Did it have algae?

KEITH: Yes, it had all kinds of problems.

KEITH: It's hard to keep the chemicals right.

CORNISH: Did you catch who did it?

KEITH: Me and my indifference to chemicals.

Well, thank you guys so much for talking with me today. We found -- we uncovered a lot of very serious news that we need to follow up on.

ROCHA: But we did not find out who sabotaged the pool.

CORNISH: We have not found that out yet.

ROCHA: Right. That's what I'm worried about.

CORNISH: We will have intelligence agencies on that shortly.

ROCHA: They put up new cameras down there all up and down the way.

CORNISH: All right, see, this is how you're getting me in trouble. Everybody else, especially those of you in New York, good luck today.

Polls are open. Go out and vote.

We've got plenty of news for you coming up, as we said, about the Reflecting Pool, about the state of the Iran talks, Rubio heading to the Middle East. Those headlines are next.

I'm Audie Cornish, and thank you for waking up with me.

vocabulary.en: the period during which someone holds a particular job or position
vocabulary.excerpt: the influence of New York City's mayor, tested just six months into his tenure.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他的任期刚满六个月。

vocabulary.en: the person who currently holds a particular office or position
vocabulary.excerpt: Two are going up against Democratic incumbents
vocabulary.example_cn: 两位挑战者正与民主党现任者竞争。

vocabulary.en: favoring or promoting social reform or new ideas
vocabulary.excerpt: I'm a longtime progressive, a Working Families Party member.
vocabulary.example_cn: 我是一名长期的进步派，工薪家庭党成员。

vocabulary.en: a person who advocates or practices socialism
vocabulary.excerpt: Can more Democratic socialists ride Mayor Mamdani's wave?
vocabulary.example_cn: 更多的民主社会主义者能乘上曼达尼市长的浪潮吗？

vocabulary.en: a temporary alliance of distinct parties, persons, or states for joint action
vocabulary.excerpt: Vox was calling this race a splitting of Mamdani's coalition
vocabulary.example_cn: Vox称这场竞选是曼达尼联盟的分裂。

vocabulary.en: a person or thing that precipitates an event or change
vocabulary.excerpt: Dr. King talked about creative tension being a catalyst for social justice.
vocabulary.example_cn: 金博士谈到创造性紧张是社会正义的催化剂。

vocabulary.en: a person or thing that is widely regarded as an object of fear
vocabulary.excerpt: AIPAC has become a real boogeyman in races.
vocabulary.example_cn: AIPAC在竞选中已成为一个真正的可怕角色。

vocabulary.en: a serious break in friendly relations
vocabulary.excerpt: It has created a political rift, both in the Democratic Party and in the Republican Party.
vocabulary.example_cn: 它在民主党和共和党内部都造成了政治裂痕。

vocabulary.en: a strong adverse reaction to something
vocabulary.excerpt: this backlash against Israel
vocabulary.example_cn: 这种对以色列的强烈反对。

vocabulary.en: to make a problem, bad situation, or negative feeling worse
vocabulary.excerpt: And exacerbated by October 7th and all that has followed since.
vocabulary.example_cn: 并且因10月7日及之后发生的一切而加剧。

vocabulary.en: a sum of money demanded or paid for the release of a captive
vocabulary.excerpt: one prior which demanded ransom
vocabulary.example_cn: 之前一封要求赎金的信。

vocabulary.en: to cancel or abandon something
vocabulary.excerpt: The Trump administration blocked from using Social Security and immigration data to scrub voter rolls.
vocabulary.example_cn: 特朗普政府被禁止使用社保和移民数据来清理选民名册。

vocabulary.en: a thorough examination and repair or reorganization
vocabulary.excerpt: a key part of the president's election overhaul plan
vocabulary.example_cn: 总统选举改革计划的关键部分。

vocabulary.en: a sheet of waterproof material used as a cover
vocabulary.excerpt: what's behind the tarp outside the Kennedy Center?
vocabulary.example_cn: 肯尼迪中心外的防水布后面是什么？

vocabulary.en: to act in accordance with a wish or command
vocabulary.excerpt: complying with a judge's ruling
vocabulary.example_cn: 遵守法官的裁决。

vocabulary.en: something, especially a structure, established to remind people of a person or event
vocabulary.excerpt: a living memorial to the late president
vocabulary.example_cn: 对已故总统的活纪念碑。

vocabulary.en: a violent or sudden change or disruption
vocabulary.excerpt: this big upheaval
vocabulary.example_cn: 这场大动荡。

vocabulary.en: a test of a performer's suitability for a role
vocabulary.excerpt: Warsh's big audition was last week.
vocabulary.example_cn: 沃什的重大考验在上周。

vocabulary.en: to analyze a sentence or text grammatically
vocabulary.excerpt: the closely parsed policy statement
vocabulary.example_cn: 被仔细解析的政策声明。

vocabulary.en: not steady or stable; shaky
vocabulary.excerpt: A wonky way of saying they will get inflation back to 2 percent.
vocabulary.example_cn: 一种不稳定的说法，表示他们将把通胀率恢复到2%。

vocabulary.en: to struggle to deal with or overcome a difficulty
vocabulary.excerpt: what the Fed has to grapple with here
vocabulary.example_cn: 美联储必须努力解决的问题。

vocabulary.en: deep respect or admiration
vocabulary.excerpt: why people still think of him with such reverence
vocabulary.example_cn: 为什么人们仍然对他如此尊敬。

vocabulary.en: to be in charge of a meeting, ceremony, or organization
vocabulary.excerpt: he presided over a period where there was huge technological change
vocabulary.example_cn: 他主持了一个技术巨变的时期。

vocabulary.en: to show or guide someone somewhere; to cause something to begin
vocabulary.excerpt: the Internet boom was going to usher in this huge era of productivity
vocabulary.example_cn: 互联网繁荣将开创一个巨大的生产力时代。

vocabulary.en: tending to persist; difficult to change
vocabulary.excerpt: inflation that is soaring now, but was already sticky before the war
vocabulary.example_cn: 现在飙升的通胀，但在战前就已经很顽固。

vocabulary.en: to increase rapidly above the usual level
vocabulary.excerpt: inflation soaring now
vocabulary.example_cn: 通胀现在飙升。

vocabulary.en: a break in the proceedings of a court, legislature, or other official body
vocabulary.excerpt: before its summer recess
vocabulary.example_cn: 在夏季休会之前。

vocabulary.en: information, especially of military or political value
vocabulary.excerpt: mass firings underway at the nation's top intel office
vocabulary.example_cn: 国家最高情报机构正在进行大规模解雇。

vocabulary.en: carefully chosen for a special purpose
vocabulary.excerpt: the president's hand-picked temporary director
vocabulary.example_cn: 总统精心挑选的临时主任。

vocabulary.en: a wind blowing in the same direction as the course of a vehicle or aircraft; a factor that helps progress
vocabulary.excerpt: he is trying to have tailwinds
vocabulary.example_cn: 他试图拥有顺风（有利因素）。

vocabulary.en: a list of cases to be heard in a court
vocabulary.excerpt: the Supreme Court expected to release some opinions for its outstanding docket
vocabulary.example_cn: 最高法院预计将就其未决案件清单发布一些意见。

vocabulary.en: the right to citizenship for anyone born in a country's territory
vocabulary.excerpt: those include birthright citizenship
vocabulary.example_cn: 这些案件包括出生公民权。

vocabulary.en: a personal stake or involvement in an undertaking
vocabulary.excerpt: the Trump administration has a vested interest in
vocabulary.example_cn: 特朗普政府在这些案件中拥有既得利益。

vocabulary.en: to criticize someone or something angrily
vocabulary.excerpt: Trump has already lashed out against the Supreme Court
vocabulary.example_cn: 特朗普已经猛烈抨击了最高法院。

vocabulary.en: to gradually destroy or weaken
vocabulary.excerpt: further erode the relationship between the executive and judiciary
vocabulary.example_cn: 进一步削弱行政与司法部门之间的关系。

vocabulary.en: hesitating or doubting; not to be relied upon
vocabulary.excerpt: legal experts have been extremely dubious about the entire time
vocabulary.example_cn: 法律专家一直对此极为怀疑。

vocabulary.en: of crucial importance in relation to the development or success of something else
vocabulary.excerpt: became this, like, pivotal thing for Democrats
vocabulary.example_cn: 成为民主党人的关键问题。

vocabulary.en: a natural liking for and understanding of someone or something
vocabulary.excerpt: focused on their affinity, communities
vocabulary.example_cn: 专注于他们的亲和力、社区。

vocabulary.en: acting in a showy or ostentatious way to attract attention
vocabulary.excerpt: this will contribute to grandstanding
vocabulary.example_cn: 这将助长哗众取宠的行为。

vocabulary.en: a person who acts obsequiously towards someone important to gain advantage
vocabulary.excerpt: I think he's the worst form of sycophant
vocabulary.example_cn: 我认为他是最糟糕的谄媚者。

vocabulary.cn: 避雷针；引人注目的人或物
vocabulary.en: a person or thing that attracts criticism or controversy
vocabulary.excerpt: Bill is a lightning rod because he doesn't have any background in intelligence
vocabulary.example_cn: 比尔是个引人争议的人物，因为他没有任何情报背景。

vocabulary.en: actions intended to provoke or disturb
vocabulary.excerpt: he actually did a lot of cage rattling within the West Wing
vocabulary.example_cn: 他确实在白宫西翼制造了很多紧张。

vocabulary.en: a crack, split, or break in something; a serious disagreement
vocabulary.excerpt: highlights a rift in the party
vocabulary.example_cn: 凸显了党内的分歧。

vocabulary.cn: 暗钱（政治捐款来源不公开）
vocabulary.en: political money whose donors are not disclosed
vocabulary.excerpt: They move millions in dark money
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们转移数百万暗钱。

vocabulary.en: the deliberate killing of a large group of people, especially those of a particular nation or ethnic group
vocabulary.excerpt: an end to genocide in Netanyahu's wars
vocabulary.example_cn: 结束内塔尼亚胡战争中的种族灭绝。

vocabulary.en: combine (two or more sets of information, texts, ideas, etc.) into one
vocabulary.excerpt: when people start conflating the actions of the Israeli government with American Jews
vocabulary.example_cn: 当人们开始将以色列政府的行为与美国犹太人混为一谈时。

vocabulary.en: hostility to or prejudice against Jewish people
vocabulary.excerpt: the rise in anti-Semitism
vocabulary.example_cn: 反犹太主义的抬头。

vocabulary.cn: 煽动者，蛊惑人心的政客
vocabulary.en: a political leader who seeks support by appealing to popular prejudices
vocabulary.excerpt: the single most dangerous demagogue in this country
vocabulary.example_cn: 这个国家最危险的煽动者。

vocabulary.en: a place or event that is likely to become a focus of conflict
vocabulary.excerpt: Israel is going to be a flashpoint
vocabulary.example_cn: 以色列将成为爆发点。

vocabulary.en: be disloyal to
vocabulary.excerpt: a party that betrays its voters and country
vocabulary.example_cn: 一个背叛其选民和国家的政党。

vocabulary.en: deliberately destroy, damage, or obstruct (something), especially for political or military advantage
vocabulary.excerpt: who sabotaged the pool
vocabulary.example_cn: 谁破坏了水池。

vocabulary.en: a type of strong adhesive or plastic
vocabulary.excerpt: Epoxy. It's tough.
vocabulary.example_cn: 环氧树脂。很坚固。

vocabulary.en: the task or area of activity officially assigned to someone
vocabulary.excerpt: It could be part of the remit.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这可能是其职责的一部分。

vocabulary.en: lack of interest, concern, or sympathy
vocabulary.excerpt: Me and my indifference to chemicals.
vocabulary.example_cn: 我和我对化学品的漠不关心。

vocabulary.en: not yet dealt with or resolved
vocabulary.excerpt: cases still left outstanding
vocabulary.example_cn: 仍未解决的案件。

vocabulary.en: in or for the intervening period; provisional
vocabulary.excerpt: He's interim.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他是临时的。

vocabulary.en: confirm or ratify (a position or appointment)
vocabulary.excerpt: get him affirmed as the new DNI
vocabulary.example_cn: 让他被确认为新的国家情报总监。

vocabulary.en: an unintended disclosure of confidential information
vocabulary.excerpt: the most leaked agency in the intelligence world
vocabulary.example_cn: 情报界泄露信息最多的机构。

vocabulary.en: a claim or dispute brought to a court of law
vocabulary.excerpt: It seems like the recipe for a lawsuit.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这似乎是引发诉讼的导火索。

vocabulary.cn: 马蹄铁政治（极端左右翼趋同）
vocabulary.en: the theory that the far left and far right are more similar to each other than to the center
vocabulary.excerpt: The horseshoe politics.
vocabulary.example_cn: 马蹄铁政治。

sentences.en: Can more Democratic socialists ride Mayor Mamdani's wave?
sentences.cn: 更多的民主社会主义者能乘上曼达尼市长的浪潮吗？
sentences.structure: 简单疑问句，主语+谓语+宾语
sentences.analysis: 主语：more Democratic socialists；谓语：ride；宾语：Mayor Mamdani's wave。'ride one's wave'为习语，意为'借助某人的势头'。

sentences.en: He's backed candidates in three House races, two are going up against Democratic incumbents, and they are hoping to ride the wave of Democratic socialists, who have been winning big races across the country.
sentences.cn: 他支持了三个众议院选区的候选人，其中两位正与民主党现任者竞争，他们希望乘上民主社会主义者的浪潮，这些人已在全美赢得重大竞选。
sentences.structure: 并列复合句，由and连接三个分句，第三个分句包含定语从句
sentences.analysis: 第一个分句：He's backed candidates；第二个分句：two are going up against incumbents；第三个分句：they are hoping to ride the wave of Democratic socialists，后接who引导的非限制性定语从句修饰Democratic socialists。

sentences.en: I'm a longtime progressive, a Working Families Party member, but yes, if by 'Democratic socialist,' do you mean do I want Medicare for all?
sentences.cn: 我是一名长期的进步派，工薪家庭党成员，但是的，如果'民主社会主义者'是指我是否想要全民医保？
sentences.structure: 复合句，包含条件状语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句：I'm a longtime progressive... but yes；条件状语从句：if by 'Democratic socialist,' do you mean...；从句中嵌套间接疑问句。

sentences.en: I am someone who has felt deeply abandoned by the establishment politics that far too often see my community as merely a statistic.
sentences.cn: 我是一个深感被建制政治抛弃的人，这种政治常常把我的社区仅仅视为一个统计数据。
sentences.structure: 主句+定语从句+定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句：I am someone；第一个定语从句：who has felt deeply abandoned by the establishment politics；第二个定语从句：that far too often see my community as merely a statistic，修饰establishment politics。

sentences.en: What we are witnessing here at this moment is the tension Dr. King talked about creative tension being a catalyst for social justice.
sentences.cn: 我们此刻目睹的正是金博士所谈论的创造性紧张，这种紧张是社会正义的催化剂。
sentences.structure: 主语从句+表语从句
sentences.analysis: 主语从句：What we are witnessing here at this moment；系动词：is；表语：the tension；后接省略that的定语从句Dr. King talked about...，其中creative tension being a catalyst为动名词复合结构。

sentences.en: I was quoting Gramsci, who said that the old world is dying and the new world struggles to be born.
sentences.cn: 我引用葛兰西的话，他说旧世界正在消亡，新世界挣扎着诞生。
sentences.structure: 主句+非限制性定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句：I was quoting Gramsci；定语从句：who said that...，其中that引导宾语从句，包含两个并列分句：the old world is dying and the new world struggles to be born。

sentences.en: I used the term to describe all those who are preventing the birth of a new world, not solely AIPAC, but frankly, super PACs at large who are spending millions of dollars in deceptive and misleading ads that are blanketing airwaves.
sentences.cn: 我用这个词来描述所有阻止新世界诞生的人，不仅仅是AIPAC，坦率地说，还有那些花费数百万美元投放欺骗性和误导性广告、充斥电波的超级政治行动委员会。
sentences.structure: 主句+定语从句+定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句：I used the term to describe all those；定语从句：who are preventing the birth of a new world；插入语：not solely AIPAC, but frankly, super PACs at large；第二个定语从句：who are spending...，其中包含that引导的定语从句修饰ads。

sentences.en: A federal judge says the system relied on inaccurate information and risks wrongly removing eligible voters.
sentences.cn: 一位联邦法官表示，该系统依赖不准确的信息，并存在错误移除合格选民的风险。
sentences.structure: 主句+宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句：A federal judge says；宾语从句：the system relied on inaccurate information and risks wrongly removing eligible voters，从句中relied和risks并列作谓语。

sentences.en: Workers began removing Trump's name from an exterior wall ten days ago, complying with a judge's ruling that the institution could only be named for John F. Kennedy as a living memorial to the late president.
sentences.cn: 十天前，工人们开始从外墙移除特朗普的名字，以遵守法官的裁决，即该机构只能以约翰·F·肯尼迪命名，作为对已故总统的活纪念碑。
sentences.structure: 主句+现在分词短语作状语+同位语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句：Workers began removing Trump's name；现在分词短语：complying with a judge's ruling；同位语从句：that the institution could only be named for John F. Kennedy as a living memorial to the late president，解释ruling的内容。

sentences.en: But the problem is exactly what you say: we have this inflation issue, it's really hard for the Fed not to raise interest rates in the face of inflation that is soaring now, but was already sticky before the war.
sentences.cn: 但问题正如你所说：我们有这个通胀问题，面对现在飙升但在战前就已经顽固的通胀，美联储很难不提高利率。
sentences.structure: 主系表结构，表语为名词性从句，后接并列句
sentences.analysis: 主句：the problem is exactly what you say；冒号后为解释说明：we have this inflation issue；后接并列句：it's really hard for the Fed not to raise interest rates in the face of inflation that is soaring now, but was already sticky before the war，其中that引导定语从句修饰inflation。

sentences.en: Secretary of State Marco Rubio heads to the Middle East; he is the first White House official to do so after the signing of the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding.
sentences.cn: 国务卿马可·鲁比奥前往中东，他是美伊谅解备忘录签署后首位这样做的白宫官员。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 分号 + 并列句（含不定式短语作定语）
sentences.analysis: 分号连接两个并列句。第二个句子中不定式短语'to do so'作定语修饰'first White House official'。

sentences.en: And quarantine finally over for the last eight American passengers exposed to hantavirus who were staying at a Nebraska facility.
sentences.cn: 最后八名接触过汉坦病毒的美国乘客的隔离终于结束，他们此前一直住在内布拉斯加州的一家设施内。
sentences.structure: 主句（省略系动词） + 过去分词短语作定语 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'quarantine finally over'，省略了'is'。过去分词短语'exposed to hantavirus'修饰'passengers'，定语从句'who were staying...'进一步修饰。

sentences.en: And this morning the Supreme Court expected to release some opinions for its outstanding docket.
sentences.cn: 今天上午，最高法院预计将就其未决案件清单发布一些意见。
sentences.structure: 主句（被动语态） + 不定式短语
sentences.analysis: 主句为'the Supreme Court expected'，被动语态，不定式短语'to release...'作主语补足语。

sentences.en: These are all opinions the Trump administration has a vested interest in.
sentences.cn: 这些都是特朗普政府拥有既得利益的意见。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 定语从句（省略关系代词）
sentences.analysis: 定语从句'the Trump administration has a vested interest in'修饰'opinions'，关系代词'that'被省略。

sentences.en: And Trump has already lashed out against the Supreme Court over decisions relating to, say, his tariff policy.
sentences.cn: 特朗普已经就涉及他的关税政策等决定猛烈抨击了最高法院。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 介词短语 + 插入语
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Trump has lashed out'，介词短语'over decisions'说明原因，'relating to...'为现在分词短语作定语，'say'为插入语。

sentences.en: It's one of those things where it's kind of in the Constitution.
sentences.cn: 这是那种有点写进宪法里的事情之一。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 定语从句'where it's kind of in the Constitution'修饰'things'，'where'相当于'in which'。

sentences.en: And I do think that we could expect him, if it goes against him, to be very unhappy and not to be quiet about that because he is never quiet when he is unhappy.
sentences.cn: 我确实认为，如果结果对他不利，我们可以预期他会非常不高兴，并且不会对此保持沉默，因为他从不高兴时从不沉默。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 宾语从句（含条件状语从句和原因状语从句）
sentences.analysis: 主句'I do think'后接宾语从句'that we could expect him...'。宾语从句中包含条件状语从句'if it goes against him'和原因状语从句'because he is never quiet...'。

sentences.en: In this case, it's two students challenging state laws, banning participation in their participation in girl's sports, arguing that it violates Title IX.
sentences.cn: 在这个案件中，两名学生挑战州法律，这些法律禁止她们参加女子体育项目，他们辩称这违反了《教育法修正案第九条》。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 现在分词短语作定语 + 现在分词短语作状语
sentences.analysis: 主句为'it's two students'，现在分词短语'challenging state laws'作定语修饰'students'，'banning...'和'arguing...'均为现在分词短语，分别作定语和伴随状语。

sentences.en: And I think that you're already seeing Republicans use transgender, whether or not the Supreme Court does this or not, you're going to see two types of ads, and we're making hundreds of ads right now in these congressional seats.
sentences.cn: 我认为你已经看到共和党人利用跨性别问题，无论最高法院是否这样做，你都会看到两种类型的广告，我们现在正在这些国会席位中制作数百个广告。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 宾语从句（含让步状语从句和并列句）
sentences.analysis: 主句'I think'后接宾语从句'that you're already seeing...'。宾语从句中包含让步状语从句'whether or not...'和并列句'you're going to see...and we're making...'。

sentences.en: There is a source telling CNN that the so-called, quote, deep state firings have begun at the Office of the Director of National Intelligence.
sentences.cn: 有消息人士告诉CNN，所谓的“深层政府”解雇行动已在国家情报总监办公室开始。
sentences.structure: There be句型 + 现在分词短语作定语 + 宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句'There is a source'，现在分词短语'telling CNN...'作定语修饰'source'，其中包含宾语从句'that the so-called...have begun'。

sentences.en: Bill Pulte, who comes from housing policy, that's not the person that anyone in the Senate seems to want.
sentences.cn: 比尔·普尔特，他来自住房政策领域，他不是参议院中任何人似乎想要的人。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 非限制性定语从句 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'that's not the person'，非限制性定语从句'who comes from housing policy'修饰'Bill Pulte'，定语从句'that anyone...seems to want'修饰'the person'。

sentences.en: And I would urge President Trump to let Clayton testify.
sentences.cn: 我会敦促特朗普总统让克莱顿作证。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 不定式短语作宾语补足语
sentences.analysis: 主句'I would urge President Trump'，不定式短语'to let Clayton testify'作宾语补足语，其中'let'后接省略to的不定式'testify'。

sentences.en: Anybody who owns the shutting down of 702 under FISA will own a future attack against the United States.
sentences.cn: 任何导致《外国情报监视法》第702条失效的人都将对未来针对美国的袭击负责。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Anybody...will own a future attack'，定语从句'who owns the shutting down...'修饰'Anybody'。

sentences.en: I think that what is happening here with Tucker Carlson, with Ted Cruz is various parts of what has been the Republican coalition figuring out what happens in a potential post-Trump reality and positioning themselves.
sentences.cn: 我认为，塔克·卡尔森和特德·克鲁兹身上发生的事情是，共和党联盟的不同部分正在弄清楚在潜在的后特朗普现实中会发生什么，并为自己定位。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 宾语从句（含主语从句和宾语从句）
sentences.analysis: 主句'I think'后接宾语从句'that what is happening...is various parts...'。宾语从句中主语从句'what is happening...'作主语，表语'various parts'后接介词短语'of what has been...'，其中'what has been...'为宾语从句。

sentences.en: And I think that it is important that when we ask ourselves how such death and destruction is happening overseas, we also name those who allow it to take place.
sentences.cn: 我认为重要的是，当我们问自己这样的死亡和破坏是如何在海外发生时，我们也要点名那些允许它发生的人。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 宾语从句（含主语从句和状语从句）
sentences.analysis: 主句'I think'后接宾语从句'that it is important that...'。其中'it'为形式主语，真正主语为'that when we ask...we also name...'，包含时间状语从句'when we ask...'和定语从句'who allow it to take place'。

topics.title: 纽约市初选与民主社会主义者
topics.content: 纽约市举行初选，市长佐兰·曼达尼支持的民主社会主义者候选人挑战现任民主党议员。曼达尼试图复制其竞选成功，但面临党内建制派与进步派的分裂。
topics.keywords: New York primary, Democratic socialists, Zohran Mamdani, progressive, incumbent

topics.title: 肯尼迪中心更名事件
topics.content: 根据法官裁决，肯尼迪中心外墙上的特朗普名字被移除，因为该中心只能以约翰·F·肯尼迪命名。此举是特朗普与肯尼迪中心法律纠纷的一部分。
topics.keywords: Kennedy Center, Trump name removal, judge ruling, living memorial

topics.title: 美联储新主席凯文·沃什的政策方向
topics.content: 新任美联储主席凯文·沃什在首次政策会议上保持利率不变，但通胀上升（4.2%）和消费者信心下降可能迫使其加息。沃什倾向于减少市场指引，回归格林斯潘风格。
topics.keywords: Federal Reserve, Kevin Warsh, inflation, interest rates, Alan Greenspan

topics.title: AIPAC对两党政治的影响
topics.content: 美国以色列公共事务委员会（AIPAC）在竞选中成为争议焦点，加剧了民主党和共和党内部在以色列政策上的分歧。塔克·卡尔森的退出也与此相关。
topics.keywords: AIPAC, Israel, political rift, Tucker Carlson, Democratic Party

topics.title: 南希·格思里失踪案新线索
topics.content: 执法部门发现一封与南希·格思里失踪相关的信件，声称她已被杀。此前曾有一封赎金信。调查人员认为这些信件可信。
topics.keywords: Nancy Guthrie, disappearance, ransom note, kidnapping

topics.title: 最高法院待决案件
topics.content: 最高法院即将就跨性别运动员、出生公民权等案件作出裁决，可能影响总统权力。
topics.keywords: Supreme Court, transgender athletes, birthright citizenship, presidential power

topics.title: Supreme Court Pending Cases
topics.content: The Supreme Court is expected to release opinions on several high-profile cases, including birthright citizenship, presidential firing authority, and transgender athletes. These cases have significant implications for the Trump administration and could affect the relationship between the executive and judiciary branches.
topics.keywords: Supreme Court, birthright citizenship, presidential firing authority, transgender athletes, Trump administration

topics.title: Bill Pulte and DNI Firings
topics.content: Bill Pulte, the acting Director of National Intelligence, has begun firing staff at the ODNI, raising concerns among Democrats and some Republicans about his lack of intelligence experience. The controversy highlights tensions between Trump's desire for retribution and the need for qualified leadership in national security.
topics.keywords: Bill Pulte, DNI, firings, intelligence community, Senate confirmation

topics.title: AIPAC and Democratic Party Rift
topics.content: AIPAC's significant campaign donations have become a flashpoint in Democratic primaries, with candidates like Mayor Zohran Mamdani refusing AIPAC funds and criticizing its influence. The issue reflects broader divisions within the party over Israel policy and the use of dark money in politics.
topics.keywords: AIPAC, Democratic Party, Israel, campaign finance, Zohran Mamdani

topics.title: Tucker Carlson Leaves Republican Party
topics.content: Tucker Carlson announced he is leaving the Republican Party, citing its loyalty to Israel over American interests. This move, along with similar sentiments from Marjorie Taylor Greene, signals a potential realignment within the conservative movement, particularly on foreign policy and the 2028 presidential race.
topics.keywords: Tucker Carlson, Republican Party, Israel, 2028 election, conservative movement

topics.title: World Cup and Cultural Exchange
topics.content: The World Cup has brought international visitors to the U.S., with positive reactions to American food and culture. The event also highlights the global appeal of soccer and the economic impact of hosting such tournaments.
topics.keywords: World Cup, cultural exchange, tourism, American food
